race.name: Les Cinglés du Mont Ventoux
race.tagline: Three ascents. Three faces. 4,400 meters of climbing. One day. The mountain they call the Beast of Provence — climbed from every side, because once is never enough.

vitals.date: June through September (summit road must be open)
vitals.date_specific: Self-scheduled — any day the D974 summit road is open, typically mid-April through mid-November. Register at least 15 days in advance (21 days during holiday periods).
terrain_types: Paved French departmental roads — D974 (Bédoin, Malaucène) and D164 (Sault)
Sustained Alpine-style climbing on all three faces
Linking roads between the three base villages
vitals.field_size: Individual challenge — no mass start. Over 20,700 verified completions since 1988.
vitals.start_time: Self-selected. Most riders begin at dawn (5-6 AM) to avoid afternoon heat.
vitals.registration: Register via clubcinglesventoux.org at least 15 days in advance. EUR 20 for the brevet card. No other entry requirements.
vitals.prize_purse: None. You receive a medal, certificate, and lifetime membership in the Club des Cinglés.
vitals.aid_stations: None provided — this is a self-supported personal challenge. Water fountains at Chalet Reynard (Bédoin side) and in the three base villages. Summit gift shop and café for water and food when open. Carry your own supplies.
vitals.cutoff_time: 24 hours — midnight to midnight. Most strong riders complete in 7-10 hours of riding time, 9-12 hours total with stops.
distance_options: The Cinglé: 3 ascents via Bédoin, Malaucène, and Sault — 137 km, ~4,400m climbing
The Bicinglé: all 3 routes climbed twice (6 ascents) in 24 hours — for the truly deranged
vitals.entry_fee: EUR 20 (~$22 USD) for the brevet card

climate.primary: Provençal summer — extreme heat at the base, Alpine conditions at the summit
climate.description: The Cinglés challenge spans three climate zones in a single ride. Base villages (Bédoin, Malaucène, Sault) sit in the Provençal lowlands where summer temperatures regularly exceed 35°C (95°F). The forested middle sections of the Bédoin and Malaucène climbs offer shade but trap humid heat. Above the treeline at approximately 1,600m, the landscape transforms into a barren, white limestone moonscape where the temperature drops 15-20°C from the base — and the Mistral wind can turn a hot day into a survival exercise. The summit at 1,912m sees wind speeds exceeding 100 km/h on 130 days per year. You will experience Mediterranean summer and near-Alpine exposure in the same hour, three times.
challenges: Extreme heat in the Provençal lowlands — 35-40°C at base villages in July-August
Mistral wind at the summit — the most relentless wind in European cycling, up to 100+ km/h
Temperature differential of 15-20°C between base and summit, experienced three times
Total sun exposure above the treeline — no shade for the final 6 km of each ascent
Cumulative dehydration across three ascents in summer heat — 7-12 liters of fluid required

terrain.primary: Paved — three distinct ascents of Mont Ventoux
terrain.surface: French departmental roads throughout. The D974 serves the Bédoin (south) and Malaucène (north) ascents; the D164 serves the Sault (east) approach. Road surfaces are well-maintained — Mont Ventoux is heavily cycled and the roads are kept in good condition. The linking roads between the three base villages (Bédoin-Malaucène: ~20 km, Malaucène-Sault: ~30 km, Sault-Bédoin: ~25 km) are rolling Provençal country roads through lavender fields and vineyards.
features: Bédoin (south): 21.5 km, 1,617m elevation, 7.4% average — the classic Tour de France ascent through dense forest then exposed moonscape
Malaucène (north): 21.2 km, 1,535m elevation, 7.2% average — equally brutal, less famous, more varied gradients
Sault (east): 25.6 km, 1,210m elevation, 4.7% average — the longest and most gradual, a false sense of mercy
Exposed limestone moonscape above 1,600m — the final 6 km on all three routes converge into the same barren, treeless summit cone
Tom Simpson memorial 1 km below the summit — cycling's most haunting landmark

gravel_god_rating.tier_note: Not a race — a self-supported personal challenge to ascend Mont Ventoux from all three sides in a single day. The Club des Cinglés has verified over 20,700 completions since 1988. Prestige=4 for Mont Ventoux itself — the Beast of Provence, sacred ground in cycling history. p4 would promote one tier but cannot promote into T1, so remains T2.
gravel_god_rating.score_note: elevation=5 for 4,400m of climbing in a single day — brutal by any standard. experience=5 for three distinct faces of cycling's most legendary mountain, including the lunar summit, the Tom Simpson memorial, and Provençal village linking roads. adventure=4 for the geographic journey around the mountain — three different approaches, three different landscapes, one barren summit. field_depth=1 because this is explicitly a solo or small-group personal challenge. race_quality=2 because there is no organization on the day — you carry a card and get stamps. value=4 because EUR 20 for a lifetime achievement and club membership is absurdly good. cultural_impact=4 for Mont Ventoux's towering significance in cycling history — Tom Simpson, the Tour de France, Petrarch's ascent in 1336.

logistics.explanation: Mont Ventoux sits in the Vaucluse department of Provence — beautiful but not trivially accessible. Marseille Provence Airport (MRS) is the closest major airport, roughly two hours by car. Avignon TGV station is closer — about 50 minutes to Bédoin — and connects via high-speed rail to Paris, Lyon, and Marseille. The base village of Bédoin has limited but charming accommodation. Malaucène and Sault are even smaller. You will need a car. The upside: this is Provence, so the logistics of getting here involve lavender fields, rosé, and some of the finest markets in France. Not exactly hardship.

length.explanation: 137 km is not an epic distance on paper. It is the vertical compression that transforms the numbers. Those 137 km contain 4,400m of climbing — one of the highest climbing-to-distance ratios in cycling. The linking roads between base villages (20-30 km each) add rolling Provençal terrain that keeps the legs honest between ascents. Most riders spend 9-12 hours total. This is not a century ride that happens to include some hills — it is a triple mountain assault with some flat bits connecting the suffering.

technicality.explanation: Paved roads throughout. Well-maintained French departmental roads that see heavy cycling traffic. The hairpins on the Bédoin and Malaucène descents require basic descending competence, and the steep gradients demand proper gear selection, but there is nothing technically demanding here. The challenge is entirely physiological and psychological. You do not need bike handling skills. You need legs and lungs and the willingness to keep climbing after your body has already asked you to stop.

elevation.explanation: 4,400 meters of climbing in a single day. That is not a typo. Three ascents of a 1,912m peak — from Bédoin (1,617m gain), Malaucène (1,535m), and Sault (1,210m) — plus the rolling linking roads between villages. For context: the Étape du Tour typically features 3,500-4,500m. A single Ventoux ascent from Bédoin is already a significant day. Doing it three times, from three different directions, with the cumulative fatigue compounding on each ascent, is one of the great climbing challenges in European cycling. The Bédoin side's 9.5 km stretch from Saint-Estève to Chalet Reynard averages over 9% with sections above 12%. You climb that once. Then you climb the north side. Then the east. The mountain does not get shorter.

climate.explanation: Provençal summer is glorious until you are climbing 4,400 meters through it. Base temperatures of 35-40°C in July and August mean the early kilometers of each ascent are a furnace. The forest sections on the Bédoin and Malaucène sides provide shade but trap humidity. Above the treeline, the sun exposure is total — white limestone reflects heat and light in every direction. And then there is the Mistral. Mont Ventoux is battered by this cold, violent northwest wind 130 days per year. A calm day at the base can be a 60 km/h headwind at the summit. The temperature swings — hot valley, cool summit, hot descent, repeat three times — demand careful layering and hydration strategy. Climate is not the primary challenge, but it is a relentless secondary one.

altitude.explanation: The summit of Mont Ventoux sits at 1,912m — the highest point in Provence, but moderate altitude by Alpine standards. On a single ascent, the altitude is negligible. But you summit three times, spending cumulative hours above 1,500m where the air is thinner and the reduced oxygen compounds existing fatigue. By the third ascent, the altitude you barely noticed on the first climb becomes noticeable. Not dangerous, but not nothing.

adventure.explanation: This is what elevates the Cinglés beyond a simple climbing challenge. Three ascents means three completely different approaches to the same mountain. From Bédoin: dense oak and pine forest, the classic Tour de France route, gradually emerging onto the barren summit. From Malaucène: steeper early ramps through shaded valleys, a quieter and more varied climb. From Sault: the long, gentle approach through lavender fields and open plateau, a slow build to the shared suffering of the final summit cone. The linking roads between villages wind through archetypal Provence — vineyards, lavender, stone villages, cicadas. You ride around an entire mountain, seeing it from every angle. The summit is the same alien moonscape each time, but the journey to reach it is different three times over.

prestige.explanation: Mont Ventoux is cycling's cathedral. The Giant of Provence. The Beast of Provence. The Bald Mountain. It has appeared in the Tour de France over 20 times. Tom Simpson died on its slopes in 1967, and the memorial 1 km below the summit is the most visited pilgrimage site in professional cycling. Petrarch climbed it in 1336 and wrote about it as a spiritual experience. The Club des Cinglés was founded in 1988 by Christian Pic, and the challenge has been completed over 20,700 times — with 348 riders achieving the Bicinglé (all three routes twice in a day). The prestige is not from a race organization or a prize list. It is from the mountain itself, and from the audacity of climbing it three times.

race_quality.explanation: There is no race. There is no organization on the day. There are no marshals, no feed zones, no timing mats, no wave starts. You register online, receive a brevet card in the post, and ride the mountain three times under your own power and your own navigation. The summit gift shop stamps your card. A café in each village stamps your card. You mail the card back to Monsieur Pic, and weeks later you receive your medal and certificate. The simplicity is the point — this is cycling at its most honest. But if you expect organized event infrastructure, you will be disappointed. You are on your own.

experience.explanation: There may be no more complete single-day cycling experience in Europe. Three ascents of the most legendary mountain in cycling, each from a different direction, each with its own character. The first ascent is anticipation and awe. The second is grim determination. The third is transcendence — or collapse. You pass the Tom Simpson memorial three times (once on each summit approach). You emerge from the treeline into the lunar landscape three times — that moment where the trees end and the white limestone begins never loses its shock. You stand at 1,912m, at the observatory tower on the bare summit, and you can see the Alps, the Mediterranean, and the Rhône Valley. Then you descend, ride through lavender fields, and climb it again. The word 'cinglé' means 'crazy' in French. By the end, you understand why.

community.explanation: The Club des Cinglés has over 15,500 members — a global community of riders who have completed the triple ascent. There is genuine camaraderie among those who have earned the card: the knowing nod from other cyclists on the mountain, the shared understanding of what three ascents demands. But the challenge itself is solitary. You ride alone or with your own group. There is no mass-start atmosphere, no shared suffering with strangers, no finish-line crowd. The community exists in retrospect — in the club membership, the medal on the wall, the story you tell. It is not the communal experience of a mass sportive.

field_depth.explanation: There is no field. There is no competition. The only person you are racing is yourself and the midnight cutoff. The 20,700+ completions span 35 years of cyclists from weekend warriors to professional riders, from 18-year-olds to septuagenarians. The Cinglés does not care about your FTP. It cares about your willingness to keep turning the pedals after 4,000 meters of climbing.

value.explanation: EUR 20. Twenty euros. For a lifetime membership in one of cycling's most exclusive clubs, a medal, a certificate, and the right to say you climbed Mont Ventoux three times in a day. No other achievement in cycling offers this ratio of suffering-to-cost. The brevet card system is beautifully analog — stamps from cafés and gift shops, mailed back for verification. There is no finisher photo package upsell, no timing chip rental, no premium entry tier. Twenty euros and the mountain. That is the deal.

expenses.explanation: The challenge itself costs EUR 20, but getting to Provence and staying there is another matter. Flights to Marseille from most European cities are affordable (EUR 50-200). Accommodation in Bédoin, Malaucène, or Sault ranges from EUR 60-150/night for gîtes and small hotels — this is rural Provence, not the Côte d'Azur. Car rental is essential. Food is excellent and reasonably priced — Provençal markets, local restaurants, bakeries. Budget EUR 500-800 for a long weekend including flights, car, accommodation, and the best rosé you have ever tasted. The mountain, at least, is free.

biased_opinion.summary: Les Cinglés du Mont Ventoux is not a race. It is not a sportive. It is not an organized event. It is a personal reckoning with the most revered mountain in cycling, repeated until either you or the mountain blinks. Three ascents — Bédoin's relentless forest grind, Malaucène's savage opening ramps, Sault's deceptively gentle approach — each delivering you to the same barren, windswept summit at 1,912m where an observatory tower stands like a lighthouse above the clouds and Tom Simpson's memorial reminds you that this mountain does not care about your suffering. The genius of the Cinglés is in its simplicity: EUR 20, a brevet card, three stamps, three summits, midnight to midnight. No marshals. No aid stations. No chip timing. Just you, the mountain, and 4,400 meters of vertical truth. The Club des Cinglés has existed since 1988 because Christian Pic understood something fundamental: some challenges do not need organization. They need audacity. The word 'cinglé' means 'crazy' in French, and by the third ascent — when your legs remember the first two and the summit looks impossibly far and the Mistral is shredding your jersey — you understand that it is not a name. It is a diagnosis.
strengths: Mont Ventoux: the Beast of Provence, cycling's most legendary mountain — climbed from all three sides in a single day
4,400m of climbing for EUR 20 — the greatest value proposition in cycling
Three completely different ascents of the same mountain: forest, exposed ramps, lavender plateau
The lunar summit above 1,600m — white limestone, no trees, no shelter, no mercy — experienced three times
Total freedom: no mass start, no waves, no schedule — you and the mountain on your own terms
weaknesses: No organization on the day: no aid stations, no marshals, no medical support — you must be fully self-sufficient
Provençal summer heat at the base (35-40°C) makes hydration and pacing critical — poor planning ends your day
The Mistral wind at the summit can turn a climb into a survival exercise without warning
No competitive element: if you need a race number and a result list, this is not your event
biased_opinion.bottom_line: Twenty euros. Three stamps. 4,400 meters of climbing. The mountain that has broken professional riders for a century. If you need someone to organize your suffering, stay home. If you are ready to organize your own, welcome to the club.

final_verdict.one_liner: Three faces. One mountain. 4,400 meters of climbing. The most honest challenge in cycling — EUR 20 and a brevet card against the Beast of Provence.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you want to experience the most complete day possible on a single mountain — three ascents, three landscapes, one barren summit that never gets less shocking. Yes if you value self-reliance and the purity of a challenge that has no finish-line arch, no announcer, no medal ceremony — just a stamped card and the knowledge of what you did. Yes if Mont Ventoux means something to you. Skip if you need organized event support, medical coverage, and feed stations. Skip if Provençal summer heat is a deal-breaker. Skip if you want to race against other people rather than against yourself.
final_verdict.alternatives: If you want an organized sportive on Mont Ventoux: try the Colnago GF Mont Ventoux (June). If you want a similar triple-ascent challenge on a different mountain: try the Alpe d'HuZes charity event. If you want massive organized climbing in France: try the Étape du Tour or La Marmotte.

logistics.airport: Marseille Provence (MRS) — 2 hours by car to Bédoin. Avignon TGV station — 50 minutes to Bédoin, with high-speed rail connections to Paris (2h40), Lyon (1h), and Marseille (30min). Nîmes (FNI) — budget airline hub, 1.5 hours to Bédoin.
logistics.lodging_strategy: Bédoin is the traditional base — a small Provençal village at the foot of the south face with cycling-specific accommodation (gîtes, B&Bs, the Bedoin Velo Lodge). Malaucène and Sault offer similar small-town accommodation. Book well in advance for June-August — this is peak Provence season and cycling tourism fills beds fast. Carpentras (20 minutes from Bédoin) offers more options. For the challenge itself, Bédoin is ideal — most riders start from the south face. Budget EUR 60-150/night.
logistics.food: Provençal cuisine at its finest — markets in Bédoin (Monday mornings) and Malaucène are legendary. Pre-ride carb loading at local restaurants: pasta, ratatouille, tian. On the mountain: carry everything. The summit gift shop/café may have water and basic food, but do not count on it. Chalet Reynard (Bédoin side, at 1,420m) has a restaurant and water tap. Post-ride: find a shaded terrace in Bédoin, order a carafe of rosé from the Ventoux AOC vineyards that surround the mountain, and contemplate what you just did to yourself.
logistics.packet_pickup: No packet pickup. Register online at clubcinglesventoux.org at least 15 days in advance (21 days during holiday periods). Your brevet card arrives by mail. Bring it on ride day.
logistics.parking: Free parking in Bédoin village. The roundabout at the start of the D974 climb is the traditional starting point. Malaucène and Sault have small village parking. No event-specific parking infrastructure — this is a personal challenge, not a mass event.

history.origin_story: In 1988, Christian Pic — a local cyclist and Ventoux devotee — formalized what determined riders had been doing informally for years: climbing Mont Ventoux from all three paved sides in a single day. He created the Club des Cinglés du Mont Ventoux to recognize and celebrate this feat of endurance and devotion. The name 'Cinglé' — meaning 'crazy' or 'screwball' in French — was chosen with full self-awareness. The concept is brilliantly simple: register, receive a brevet card, climb the mountain three times, get your card stamped at the summit and in each base village, mail it back. Since 1988, over 20,700 riders have officially completed the challenge, and 348 have achieved the Bicinglé — all three routes climbed twice (six ascents) in a single day. The club predates the modern sportive industry by decades. There are no corporate sponsors, no timing chips, no premium entry tiers. There is a mountain, a card, and the question of whether you are crazy enough.
notable_moments: 1988: Christian Pic founds the Club des Cinglés du Mont Ventoux, formalizing the triple-ascent challenge
1967: Tom Simpson dies 1 km below the summit during the Tour de France — the memorial becomes cycling's most sacred site
Mont Ventoux has appeared in the Tour de France over 20 times — its summit finishes are among the most dramatic in cycling history
1336: Petrarch climbs Mont Ventoux and writes about the experience — often cited as the birth of modern mountaineering and tourism
Over 20,700 verified Cinglé completions and 348 Bicinglés as of 2025
15,500+ members of the Club des Cinglés worldwide
history.reputation: The honest challenge. No frills, no timing, no organization — just a mountain, a card, and 4,400 meters of climbing. Cycling's most elegant test of will.

course_description.character: Three ascents of the same mountain from three different directions, linked by rolling Provençal country roads through lavender fields and vineyards. Each climb has a distinct personality: Bédoin is the classic — dense forest giving way to exposed moonscape, the route the Tour de France made famous. Malaucène is the underestimated equal — steep early ramps, varied terrain, fewer cyclists. Sault is the long, deceptive approach — gentle gradients through lavender plateau that lull you before the final shared summit cone brutalizes you. All three routes converge in the last 6 km, where the trees vanish and the white limestone begins — a barren, lunar landscape that looks like no other place in cycling. The observatory tower at 1,912m is visible from kilometers away, growing imperceptibly closer with each pedal stroke. You pass the Tom Simpson memorial three times. You stand at the summit three times. And three times, you descend.

suffering_zones.label: Bédoin — The Classic (21.5 km, 1,617m, 7.4%)
suffering_zones.desc: The climb the Tour de France built. From the roundabout in Bédoin at 300m, the first 5.5 km to Saint-Estève are deceptively gentle — 4.4% through open terrain, legs still fresh, the mountain looming above. Then the road enters the forest and the gradient hits you like a wall. The 9.5 km from Saint-Estève to Chalet Reynard average over 9%, with sustained sections above 11-12%. Dense pine and oak forest blocks the wind but traps the heat. This is where the Bédoin climb earns its reputation — nearly 10 km of relentless 9%+ gradient with no respite. At Chalet Reynard (1,420m), you emerge from the forest into the moonscape. The final 6 km to the summit are exposed, windswept, and psychologically devastating — white limestone in every direction, the observatory tower visible but never arriving fast enough. The gradient eases to 7% before a vicious 10% kick in the final 1.5 km past the Tom Simpson memorial.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Forest: 9%+ sustained for 9.5 km. Summit cone: exposed limestone, 7% easing to 10% final kick

suffering_zones.label: Malaucène — The Northern Face (21.2 km, 1,535m, 7.2%)
suffering_zones.desc: Where Bédoin has a defined crux in the forest, Malaucène spreads its cruelty more evenly. The climb begins steeply from the village — 10%+ in the opening kilometers — then alternates between 5% recovery sections and 10%+ ramps. This inconsistency is psychologically punishing: you never find a rhythm. The road is shadier and more sheltered from the Mistral than the Bédoin side, but the constant gradient changes prevent any settled climbing pace. On your second ascent of the day, this unpredictability becomes maddening. The final kilometers share the same moonscape approach from the north — equally barren, equally exposed, equally magnificent. The summit observatory appears from a different angle but is no less imposing.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Variable: alternating 5-10%+ ramps, no sustained rhythm. Same exposed summit approach

suffering_zones.label: Sault — The False Mercy (25.6 km, 1,210m, 4.7%)
suffering_zones.desc: Sault is the longest route and the gentlest — and by the time you climb it, neither fact helps. The approach from this eastern plateau through lavender fields and sparse forest at an average of 4.7% feels manageable for the first 18 km. The legs are grateful. The gradient is kind. The Provençal landscape is beautiful. This is a trap. The final 6 km — the shared summit section where all three routes converge — rears up to the same 7-10% exposed moonscape that punished you twice already. On legs that have already climbed 3,150m, these final 6 km are as hard as anything on the Bédoin side. The gentleness of the Sault approach has done nothing but delay the inevitable. The summit observatory appears for the third time. You will never be more relieved to see an ugly building.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Gentle: 4.7% average for 18 km, then the shared summit cone at 7-10%
suffering_zones.named_section: The Lavender Approach / The Final Moonscape

course_description.signature_challenge: The third summit. The first ascent is excitement — the mountain is fresh, the legs are willing, the scenery is extraordinary. The second ascent is determination — the mountain is familiar, the legs are heavy, the summit seems farther away. The third ascent is reckoning. Your body has already climbed 3,150 meters. The Sault approach's gentle gradients offer false hope. The final 6 km of exposed limestone — the same white moonscape you have already climbed twice — must be climbed one more time. The observatory tower is visible. The Tom Simpson memorial is ahead. The wind is blowing. The third summit is where the Cinglés challenge becomes exactly what its name promises: crazy.

guide_variables.race_terrain: Three paved ascents of Mont Ventoux — Bédoin (south), Malaucène (north), Sault (east) — linked by rolling Provençal roads
guide_variables.race_weather: Provençal summer: 30-40°C at base, 15-25°C at summit, Mistral wind possible at any time
race_challenges: 4,400 meters of climbing in a single day across three distinct ascents
Provençal summer heat at base villages (35-40°C in July-August)
Exposed, treeless summit above 1,600m — Mistral wind up to 100+ km/h
Self-supported: no aid stations, no marshals, no medical support on course
Cumulative fatigue: the third ascent must be climbed on legs that have already gained 3,150m

non_negotiables.requirement: Sustained climbing endurance — 4+ hours of continuous climbing in a single session
non_negotiables.why: The Cinglés demands 7-10 hours of climbing time. Each ascent takes 60-120 minutes depending on the route and your fitness. Your body must be adapted to repeated, prolonged climbing efforts with incomplete recovery between them. Build toward a training day with 3,000m+ of climbing before attempting the challenge.

non_negotiables.requirement: Heat acclimatization and hydration strategy tested in training
non_negotiables.why: Provençal summer heat (35-40°C) combined with 4,400m of climbing creates extreme fluid loss. Most riders need 7-12 liters across the day. You must know your sweat rate, your sodium needs, and where you will refill bottles. The mountain has limited water sources — Chalet Reynard, summit café (if open), and village fountains. Run out of water above the treeline and your day is over.

non_negotiables.requirement: Full reconnaissance of all three routes — or detailed route plan with GPS
non_negotiables.why: The linking roads between Bédoin, Malaucène, and Sault are not marked or marshaled. Navigation errors add distance and climbing to an already maximal day. Know the route between villages, know where to refill water, know where the cafés are. The recommended order (Bédoin-Malaucène-Sault) saves the easiest ascent for last, but choose based on your own strengths and the day's weather.

non_negotiables.requirement: Descending competence on steep, winding mountain roads — at speed, on tired legs
non_negotiables.why: You will descend Mont Ventoux three times — on roads with hairpin bends, steep gradients, and potentially strong crosswinds. Each descent is 20-25 km. On tired legs, after hours of climbing, your reaction time and bike handling deteriorate. Practice long descents on fatigued legs. This is where accidents happen.

videos.title: Becoming Cinglés du Mont Ventoux
videos.description: In the Ventoux area for a week-long training camp, our group tackles the famous challenge: three times up and down Mont Ventoux in the same day, once by each of the three routes.
videos.curation_reason: Focused coverage of the classic Cinglés challenge with group experience tackling all three routes in one day.

videos.title: Ma "Cinglés du Mont Ventoux" (Montée du Ventoux par les 3 faces en 9h17)
Quelle est la meilleure façon de s'entraîner pour les longs cols ?
videos.description:
Les grands cols tels que l’Iseran, Le Galibier ou des sommets mythiques tels que l’Alpe d’huez, le Mont Ventoux nous font tous rêver. Mais la pratique du vélo est complexe et nécessite des qualités particulières, des braquets adaptés ainsi qu’un entraînement adapté.
Mais comment s’entraîner pour passer une belle journée sur ces routes qui montent à pourcentages élevés pendant plusieurs kilomètres quand on habite dans une région à dominante plate ?
____________________

GCN en Français c’est LA chaîne incontournable sur le cyclisme destinée aux francophones. GCN en Français fédère l'ensemble des cyclistes francophones passionnés et célèbre l'ensemble du monde du cyclisme.

Grâce à nos vidéos et leur contenu captivant, les fans de cyclisme ont à leur disposition des tutoriels d'experts, des vidéos techniques, d'entraînement, de courses professionnelles, des informations sur les technologies dernier cri, des reportages exclusifs dans les coulisses des courses, de l'humour, du divertissement et bien plus encore.

Présentée par des anciens coureurs du peloton pro, GCN en Français c'est une introspection experte dans le monde du cyclisme; mais le plus important, c'est la passion et l'enthousiasme que nos fans nous apportent grâce à leur engagement en continu – c'est à dire, toutes celles et ceux qui représentent l'ensemble de la communauté GCN en Français.
Nous vous présentons en avant première les toutes dernières avancées technologiques en matière de cyclisme, nous vous emmenons également sur les plus belles routes et les plus beaux cols, de plus, vous obtenez un accès exclusif aux événements et courses.

Bienvenue sur GCN en Français.

videos.title: Gran Fondo au Mont Ventoux
videos.description:
Après une montée en solo et l’aventure de la Cinglé, j’ai choisi cette fois de relever le défi du Gran Fondo au Mont Ventoux. Départ de Bédoin, avec un premier passage dans les magnifiques Gorges de la Nesque, suivi d’un arrêt ravitaillement à Sault pour reprendre des forces avant la montée vers le Chalet Reynard.

L’ascension finale vers le sommet a été rude. Chaleur ? Fringale ? Difficile à dire, mais j’ai pris un gros coup de mou. Heureusement, un peu de sucre, un peu de sel, et c’est reparti ! Dernier arrêt sucré-salé à Malaucène avant de franchir le Col de la Madeleine. Retour à Bédoin, fatigué mais satisfait… ouf !

Chapitre 1 : on démarre

00:00 - Départ de notre Gran Fondo
01:01 - Discussion sur la parcours avec Archi
02:39 - On quitte Bédoin
03:36 - On arrive à Villes-sur-Auzon

Chapitre 2 : les Gorges de la Nesque

04:40 - Les Gorges de la Nesque, c'est parti pour 18 kilomètres
10:12 - Vue sur le Ventoux
13:58 - Vue sur les Gorges
15:23 - Un motard au casque "trop stylé"
16:37 - Archi, sans les mains !
19:35 - On s'accroche à un peloton d'avignonnais 
23:02 - Petit coup d'accélérateur pour les avignonnais et Archi dans les derniers hectomètres de l'ascension
29:26 - Le premier tunnel 
32:45 - Encore un tunnel
33:59 - Le dernier tunnel 
35:25 - Enfin le sommet, on peut descredre vers Sault
35:40 - Monieux
36:19 - Passage au-dessus de la Nesque à La Loge
39:51 - On arrive enfin à Sault !
41:53 - On s'offre une petite pause

Chapitre 3 : le Ventoux (depuis Sault)

42:06 - On est reparti, direction le sommet du Mont Ventoux
42:23 - Archi s'evole vers le sommet
44:12 - Le Chalet Reynard 
44:30 - Petit point 
45:21 - La Fontaine de la Grave
45:58 - Panne sèche  
46:35 - Les derniers mètres
48:19 - Le sommet du Mont Ventoux !
48:30 - Il est déjà temps de redescendre
49:28 - Malaucène
50:14 - Le sommet du Col de la Madeleine
58:09 - Le mot de la fin ...
59:40 - Bédoin
 
Le parcours : https://www.strava.com/activities/12043549545

Mon profil Strava : https://www.strava.com/athletes/jameson_clarence
Mon compte Instagram : https://www.instagram.com/JamesonClarence/
Le groupe Facebook : https://www.facebook.com/groups/325228318818174
videos.curation_reason: Recent first-person ride experience with detailed course description including Gorges de la Nesque section and personal challenges faced during the ascent.

videos.title: Bédoin - Mont Ventoux (again) 05.06.2023 (2,7k Large 60fps).
videos.description:
Équipe 17 et 71 ans "a mûri" et devient 21 et 75 ans. En 2019 nous étions tellement émerveillés par les environs du Mont Ventoux que nous avons voulu répéter l'expérience en l'abordant cette fois des trois côtés.  
Entre-temps nous avons acquis d'expérience en escaladant Alpe-d'Huez, Galibier, Croix-de-Fer, Tourmalet, Hautacam, Aubisque... ce qui va nous aider certainement, ainsi que les entraînement et les vélos mieux adaptés à ce genre d'efforts.
On commence par le versant sud, celui qu'on a déjà fait en 2019, le mythique et le plus difficile. Départ tôt, la pluie annoncée au sommet dès 11h. 
Malheureusement le beau temps de 2019 n'était pas au rendez-vous.  Filmé par le plus jeune de l'équipe (21 an.
Par contre nous avons monté plus vite :
Fils (21 ans)
5 juin 2023 2:07:27 10,2 km/h
2 juin 2019 2:56:12 7,4 km/h
Old man (75 ans)
5 juin 2023  2:47:47 7,8 km/h
2 juin 2019  3:22:01 6,4 km/h
@ArtSportNature
